user.description: Set up Necromancer. Add the devices you want guarded afterwards.

init.title: PoE ports

add_port.title: PoE port
add_port.description: A PoE port: how it's switched, how the connected device is recognised, how its online status is checked, and its timing.

data_description.label: A name for this port, e.g. its switch port number or the device it powers.

power.name: Power switch
power.description: Switch that cuts power (PoE) on the port.

data_description.actuator: e.g. a smart plug or PoE switch — a `switch` or `input_boolean`.

identity.name: Recognition
identity.description: The id of the device on this port. A Necromancer guard with the Auto-PoE strategy matches its monitored device's id against it and cycles this port. Provide the id one way — from an entity (live), or a static label.

data_description.id_entity: An entity whose state is the id of the device on this port — for ports where the device can change.
data_description.id_attribute: Read this attribute of the entity instead of its state (leave empty to use the state).
data_description.id_static: A static value identifying the device on this port — e.g. a MAC, IP or a name you assign. Use this instead of an entity, not together with one.

status.name: Online status
status.description: How Necromancer sees whether the port is online — to confirm the power-cycle worked.

data_description.status_entity: An entity that reports whether the port is online — e.g. a ping or reachability sensor.
data_description.status_attribute: Leave empty to use the entity's own state.

timing.name: Timing
timing.description: Wait times while powering the port off and on.

data_description.off_on_delay: How long to stay off before powering back on.
data_description.off_timeout: How long to wait for the port to read offline after cutting power.
data_description.on_timeout: How long to wait for the port to come back online after restoring power.

edit_port.title: Which port to edit?
edit_port.description: Pick the port you want to edit.

delete_port.title: Which port to delete?
delete_port.description: Pick the port you want to remove.

import_ports.title: Import ports
import_ports.description: Paste a list of ports as YAML. “Merge” adds new ports and updates existing ones with the same name; “Replace” overwrites the whole list. Tip: export first to see the exact format.

export_ports.title: Export ports
export_ports.description: Choose which ports to export — all are selected by default.

export_result.title: Exported ports
export_result.description:
Copy the YAML below — edit it and paste it back via “Import ports”.

```
{yaml}
```

error.id_conflict: Set the device id one way — an entity or a static label, not both.
error.attribute_needs_entity: An attribute needs an id entity to read it from. Pick an entity or clear the attribute.

error.no_self_link: Necromancer's own devices can't be linked. Pick a different device.
error.duplicate_name: A guard with this name already exists. Pick a unique name so the status entities stay unambiguous.
error.action_required: This recovery needs an action — a guard with no action can only escalate.

user.title: Health Source
user.description: You're adding a Necromancer guard. How should Necromancer detect the monitored device's Health State — from an entity's state, or from a Jinja template?

reconfigure.title: Health Source
reconfigure.description: How should Necromancer detect the monitored device's Health State — from an entity's state, or from a Jinja template?

device.title: Health State & Device
device.description: Give the Necromancer guard a name and define how its Health State is detected.

data.name: Name
data.entity_id: Entity that shows the Health State

data_description.name: The name of this Necromancer guard; its status entities are named after it.
data_description.entity_id: The entity that reports the monitored device's state, e.g. a ping or reachability sensor.
data_description.attribute: Leave empty to use the entity's own state.
data_description.on_value: e.g. `on`, `home`, `online`. Any value other than this or the faulty value counts as `unknown` and triggers nothing.
data_description.template: Return `true`, `on`, `1` or `yes` for OK; `false`, `off`, `0` or `no` for faulty — e.g. a comparison like CPU below 90, or `is_state(...)`. Anything else (`unavailable`, `unknown`, `none`, a blank result, or a render error) counts as `unknown` and triggers nothing (no false alarm). Re-checked when referenced entities change. Point it at the monitored device — not at this guard's own status/health entities, or you create a feedback loop.
data_description.device_id: Optional. Adds this Necromancer guard's status entities to the chosen Home Assistant device, so they appear on its device page.

strategy.title: Recovery strategy
strategy.description: How should the monitored device be brought back when it fails? On the next screen a Health Check toggle (on by default) decides whether the fix is verified against the monitored device's health before going quiet; with it off, the action is assumed to have worked.

switch.title: Power-cycle a switch
switch.description: Powers a switch off and on when the monitored device fails.

data.switch_entity: Switch to power off and on (e.g. a smart plug)

data_description.switch_entity: The switch to power off and on — e.g. a smart plug the monitored device is plugged into.
data_description.off_on_delay: How long to stay off before switching back on.

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a Health Check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the monitored device is healthy again

data_description.debounce: How long a fault must persist before Necromancer acts — brief blips are absorbed and don't trigger a recovery.
data_description.health_check: On (default): the recovery is verified against the monitored device's Health State before the guard goes quiet. Off: the action is assumed to have worked and goes directly to cooldown.
data_description.boot_window: With Health Check on: how long to wait after the action for the monitored device to read healthy again.
data_description.cooldown: A quiet period after a successful recovery before the guard watches again.
data_description.max_attempts: How many times to retry before escalating — when verifying (Health Check on), or when the action itself errors.

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything); Necromancer passes the alert text and context as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made message, or build your own. Variables: message, name, event_text, event, attempt, max, attempts, reason. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

linked_guards.name: Linked guards
linked_guards.description: Group this guard with other recovery guards so they coordinate instead of recovering in parallel.

data.notify_follower_success: Report success even when this guard follows a group recovery

data_description.linked_guards: When any guard in the group recovers, the others follow — they wait and re-check afterwards instead of recovering in parallel. Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.
data_description.notify_follower_success: On: a guard that recovered only by following the group (it took no action itself) still reports success. Off: only its own recovery counts.

reload.name: Restart device integration
reload.description: After a recovery, reload the assigned device's integration before re-checking.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a recovery

data_description.reload_entry: Reload the assigned device's integration after the recovery action, before the Health Check runs — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device for it to read healthy again.
data_description.reload_delay: How long to wait after the reload before re-checking, giving the integration time to reconnect.

action.title: Run an action
action.description: Runs your action when the monitored device fails.

recovery_action.name: Recovery action
recovery_action.description: What to run to bring the monitored device back. An action can be anything Home Assistant can do — a service or script call, an SSH/shell_command, a rest_command or webhook, or a multi-step sequence with delays, waits and conditions. It runs blocking; with Health Check on, Necromancer then waits (up to the boot window) for the monitored device to report OK again. Variables: attempt, max, name, guard_entity_id.

data.action: Action (e.g. a script, service, SSH, webhook)

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a Health Check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the monitored device is healthy again

data_description.debounce: How long a fault must persist before Necromancer acts — brief blips are absorbed and don't trigger a recovery.
data_description.health_check: On (default): the recovery is verified against the monitored device's Health State before the guard goes quiet. Off: the action is assumed to have worked and goes directly to cooldown.
data_description.boot_window: With Health Check on: how long to wait after the action for the monitored device to read healthy again.
data_description.cooldown: A quiet period after a successful recovery before the guard watches again.
data_description.max_attempts: How many times to retry before escalating — when verifying (Health Check on), or when the action itself errors.

You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything); Necromancer passes the alert text and context as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. reload.name: Restart device integration
reload.description: After a recovery, reload the assigned device's integration before re-checking.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a recovery

data_description.reload_entry: Reload the assigned device's integration after the recovery action, before the Health Check runs — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device for it to read healthy again.
data_description.reload_delay: How long to wait after the reload before re-checking, giving the integration time to reconnect.

actions.title: Off/on actions
actions.description: Runs the off action, waits, then the on action when the monitored device fails.

recovery_action.name: Recovery actions
recovery_action.description: Two sequences with a wait between: the off action, then the on action. Each can be any Home Assistant action — service or script, SSH, rest_command, webhook, sequence. Variables: attempt, max, name, guard_entity_id; any variable you set in the off action is also readable in the on action, so you can capture state before cutting power and restore it afterwards — no helper entity needed.

data.off_action: Off action (cut power / turn the monitored device off)
data.on_action: On action (restore power / turn the monitored device on)

data_description.off_on_delay: How long to wait between the off and on actions.

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a Health Check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the monitored device is healthy again

data_description.debounce: How long a fault must persist before Necromancer acts — brief blips are absorbed and don't trigger a recovery.
data_description.health_check: On (default): the recovery is verified against the monitored device's Health State before the guard goes quiet. Off: the action is assumed to have worked and goes directly to cooldown.
data_description.boot_window: With Health Check on: how long to wait after the action for the monitored device to read healthy again.
data_description.cooldown: A quiet period after a successful recovery before the guard watches again.
data_description.max_attempts: How many times to retry before escalating — when verifying (Health Check on), or when the action itself errors.

notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything); Necromancer passes the alert text and context as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made message, or build your own. Variables: message, name, event_text, event, attempt, max, attempts, reason. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

data_description.reload_delay: How long to wait after the reload before re-checking, giving the integration time to reconnect.

poe_port.title: Auto-PoE — find the port
poe_port.description: Finds the matching PoE port by the monitored device's id and powers it off and on. Manage the ports under the integration's "Configure".

data.expected_id: Device id (e.g. MAC, IP or name)

data_description.expected_id: The id that matches the monitored device to a configured PoE port — its MAC, IP or static label.

behavior.name: Behaviour
behavior.description: When Necromancer reacts, and — with a Health Check — how often it retries before giving up. The defaults are usually fine.

data.boot_window: Wait until the monitored device is healthy again

data_description.debounce: How long a fault must persist before Necromancer acts — brief blips are absorbed and don't trigger a recovery.
data_description.health_check: On (default): the recovery is verified against the monitored device's Health State before the guard goes quiet. Off: the action is assumed to have worked and goes directly to cooldown.
data_description.boot_window: With Health Check on: how long to wait after the action for the monitored device to read healthy again.
data_description.cooldown: A quiet period after a successful recovery before the guard watches again.
data_description.max_attempts: How many times to retry before escalating — when verifying (Health Check on), or when the action itself errors.

You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything); Necromancer passes the alert text and context as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made message, or build your own. Variables: message, name, event_text, event, attempt, max, attempts, reason. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

linked_guards.description: Group this guard with other recovery guards so they coordinate instead of recovering in parallel.

data.notify_follower_success: Report success even when this guard follows a group recovery

data_description.linked_guards: When any guard in the group recovers, the others follow — they wait and re-check afterwards instead of recovering in parallel. Linking is mutual and transitive: link to a guard that already has links and you join its whole group; to leave, clear all of them.

reload.description: After a recovery, reload the assigned device's integration before re-checking.

data.reload_entry: Restart the assigned device's integration after a recovery

data_description.reload_entry: Reload the assigned device's integration after the recovery action, before the Health Check runs — useful when Home Assistant must reconnect to the device for it to read healthy again.
data_description.reload_delay: How long to wait after the reload before re-checking, giving the integration time to reconnect.

notify.title: Notification
notify.description: What should happen when a problem is detected.

data_description.debounce: How long a fault must persist before the notification fires — brief blips are absorbed.

notification.name: Notification
notification.description: Optional — run an action when this guard hits a problem, recovery or escalation. You provide the action (a phone push, a TTS announcement, a light, a script — anything); Necromancer passes the alert text and context as variables, so you decide whether and how to be told. Take the ready-made message, or build your own. Variables: message, name, event_text, event, attempt, max, attempts, reason. Reference a variable with double curly braces in your action.

repair_poe_port.name: Repair PoE port
repair_poe_port.description: Resolve a device id to its PoE port and power-cycle it. Blocking and serialised per port, so multiple guards or automations coordinate on a single cycle instead of fighting over the same port.

id.name: Device id
id.description: The id that identifies the device on its PoE port — the MAC, IP or static label configured for that port.

reset.name: Reset
reset.description: Clear an escalated guard back to normal and re-check it. If the monitored device is still unhealthy the guard tries to recover again; if it has recovered it simply resumes watching.

snooze.name: Snooze
snooze.description: Temporarily suspend a guard for a set duration — it ignores the monitored device's health and raises no alerts (e.g. during planned maintenance), then resumes automatically.

duration.name: Duration
duration.description: How long to suspend the guard before it automatically resumes.

unsnooze.name: Unsnooze
unsnooze.description: Resume a snoozed guard immediately instead of waiting for its snooze to elapse.

notify_guard.name: Notify via guard
notify_guard.description: Send a custom message through a guard's own notify action — for recovery scripts that want to report their own progress on the same channel the guard already uses.

message.name: Message
message.description: The text to send. Exposed to the notify action as the `message` and `event_text` variables; the guard name is the title.

event.name: Event key
event.description: Optional routing key exposed as the `event` variable (default: `custom`), so your notify action can branch on it.

event_text.name: Event text
event_text.description: Optional name-less text exposed as `event_text` (defaults to the message) — for titles/TTS that set the name separately.

attempt.name: Attempt
attempt.description: Optional attempt count exposed as `attempt`; also derives the plural-correct `attempts` phrase.

snooze_all.name: Snooze all
snooze_all.description: Snooze every guard for a duration — system-wide maintenance mode. Each guard ignores health and raises no alerts, then auto-resumes. Guards mid-recovery are skipped (best-effort).

duration.name: Duration
duration.description: How long to suspend all guards before they auto-resume.

check_health.name: Check Health
check_health.description: Evaluate a guard's health right now and return it (`ok` / `unhealthy` / `unknown`) — for a recovery script to re-use the guard's own Health Check instead of rebuilding it.

guard.name: Guard
guard.description: The guard's status entity — the value a recovery action receives as `guard_entity_id`.

wait_for_health.name: Wait for Health
wait_for_health.description: Wait until a guard reports healthy again, or the timeout elapses. Returns `health`, `timed_out` and `waited_s` — so a staged recovery can verify between tiers using the guard's own Health Check.

guard.name: Guard
guard.description: The guard's status entity — the value a recovery action receives as `guard_entity_id`.

timeout.name: Timeout
timeout.description: Seconds to wait for health to read `OK`. Defaults to the guard's boot window.

check_first.name: Check first
check_first.description: Evaluate once before waiting and return immediately if already healthy (default: `on`).

snooze_during_recovery.message: Cannot snooze {name} while it is actively recovering — wait for the recovery to finish.

unknown_guard.message: {entity_id} is not a Necromancer guard status entity — pass the guard's status sensor, the guard_entity_id a recovery action receives.

health_entity_missing.title: {name}: health entity is missing
health_entity_missing.description: The guard "{name}" watches `{entity}`, which doesn't exist — so the guard is blind and can't detect a problem. Reconfigure the guard to watch an entity that exists, or remove the guard.

health_entity_disabled.title: {name}: health entity is disabled
health_entity_disabled.description: The guard "{name}" watches `{entity}`, which is disabled — so the guard is blind. Re-enable `{entity}`, or reconfigure the guard to watch a different entity.

health_template_blind.title: {name}: health template reads only missing entities
health_template_blind.description: Every entity the health template of "{name}" reads is missing or disabled, so the guard is blind. Reconfigure the guard's Health Template to reference entities that exist.

health_template_missing_entity.title: {name}: health template references a missing entity
health_template_missing_entity.description: The health template of "{name}" references `{entity}`, which doesn't exist — the template defaults it, so the guard reads a value that may not reflect the device and can recover or escalate on missing data. Add the entity, or handle a missing value in the template (for example with `has_value()`).

recovery_action_invalid.title: {name}: recovery can't run
recovery_action_invalid.description: The recovery of "{name}" can't run: {error}. Reconfigure the guard's recovery — a valid service/action, or (for Auto-PoE) at least one configured port.

port_no_id.title: PoE port "{label}" has no device id
port_no_id.description: The PoE port "{label}" has neither a static label nor an id-entity, so it can never be matched to a device — any guard expecting it stays blocked. Open the integration's options, edit port "{label}", and set either a static label or an id-entity (a state or an attribute).

port_entity_missing.title: PoE port "{label}": an entity is missing
port_entity_missing.description: The PoE port "{label}" references `{entity}`, which doesn't exist — so its power-cycle can't run. Open the integration's options, edit port "{label}", and point its actuator / status entity at one that exists.
